text: Authentication itself is fine (no password prompt, no rejection), so this isn't a key/AllowUsers-style problem. The connection succeeds; it's what happens *after* login that's wrong. Look at the whole sshd config, not just the top: `cat /etc/ssh/sshd_config`, all the way to the end.

Look for a `Match User deploy` block. Conditional blocks like this apply extra directives only to sessions matching the condition, and they're easy to lose track of once they're not right at the top of the file.

It sets `ForceCommand internal-sftp` for `deploy`. `ForceCommand` silently replaces *any* command the client asks to run with the configured one, regardless of what was actually requested -- so `ssh deploy@host 'echo SSH_OK'` runs an SFTP subsystem instead, not `echo`. That block was written for a different, genuinely sftp-only account and ended up matching `deploy` too.
Remove the `Match User deploy` block (or narrow it back to the account it was actually meant for), then validate and reload: `sudo sshd -t && sudo pkill sshd && sudo /usr/sbin/sshd`.
